function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Viasat's most recent 10-K discloses satellite depreciation as a significant line item within its space segment, and Viasat has historically reported negative or near-zero segment operating income despite positive EBITDA, precisely because satellite useful-life assumptions drive a large non-cash depreciation charge. Using SpaceX's disclosed Connectivity segment operating income and Segment Adjusted EBITDA for FY2025, calculate the implied depreciation and amortization add-back embedded in the EBITDA bridge. Compare this gap directionally to Viasat's disclosed satellite depreciation as a percentage of satellite-segment revenue. What does the comparison reveal about how LEO constellation economics — with higher satellite counts, shorter useful lives, and continuous replenishment — structurally differ from GEO satellite depreciation profiles, and why does this make EBITDA-to-operating-income conversion particularly important when evaluating LEO broadband operators as a class?

results.content:
# Connectivity Segment FY2025 Financial Results

## Key Figures
- **Connectivity Segment Operating Income (Income from Operations):** **$4,423 million**
- **Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA:** **$7,168 million**

## Reconciliation: Segment Operating Income → Segment Adjusted EBITDA (Year Ended December 31, 2025)

| (in millions) | Connectivity |
|---|---|
| Income from operations | $4,423 |
| Add: Depreciation and amortization | $2,376 |
| Add: Share-based compensation | $369 |
| Add: Restructuring charges | $— |
| Add: Impairment | $— |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$7,168** |

**Verification:** $4,423 + $2,376 + $369 + $0 + $0 = **$7,168 million** ✓

## Additional Context
- This represented year-over-year growth of **120.4%** in operating income and **86.2%** in Segment Adjusted EBITDA versus 2024 (when operating income was $2,006 million and Adjusted EBITDA was $3,849 million).
- The growth was primarily driven by higher revenue from consumer and enterprise customers, partially offset by higher depreciation of capitalized launch and satellite costs (due to increased Starlink flights), marketing and international expansion costs, and R&D for next-generation product development.
- Connectivity segment revenue for FY2025 was **$11,387 million** (up 49.8% YoY), primarily driven by Starlink.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, what does the filing disclose about satellite useful lives, depreciation policies, satellite replenishment, and the LEO constellation economics? Also, what is the total depreciation and amortization expense for the consolidated entity in FY2025?

results.content:
# Satellite Useful Lives, Depreciation Policies, Replenishment & LEO Economics

## Satellite Useful Lives

The filing discloses two specific useful-life categories for satellites:

- **Broadband satellites: 5-year useful life**
- **First-generation mobile satellites: 3-year useful life**

Useful lives are estimated based on **engineering studies, historical on-orbit performance, propellant life, utilization patterns, design enhancements across generations, and planned transitions to newer satellite technology**. Management periodically reviews these estimates with engineering and operations teams and revises them as additional data becomes available.

Additional factors considered for satellite assets include **on-orbit performance, orbit-raise timing, expected service capability, and the evolution of constellation density and technology**.

## Depreciation Policies

| Asset Category | Depreciation Method |
|---|---|
| General PP&E | Straight-line over estimated useful lives |
| Flight vehicles & spacecraft | Over expected number of average flights per vehicle |
| Leasehold improvements | Shorter of useful life or lease term |
| Maintenance & repairs | Expensed as incurred |
| Significant refurbishments/enhancements | Capitalized and depreciated ratably over identified useful life |

When useful lives or expected remaining flights are shorter or longer than originally estimated, the Company **adjusts the rate of depreciation** to reflect the revised estimates.

## Satellite Replenishment & LEO Constellation Economics

The filing reveals several economic dynamics of the LEO constellation model:

1. **Short useful lives drive high depreciation burden** — With broadband satellites depreciated over just 5 years, the Connectivity segment faces substantial recurring depreciation as the constellation is continually replenished.

2. **Sensitivity is material** — A one-year change in the average useful life of satellite assets would have an approximately **$480 million impact on operating income for FY2025** and **$170 million for Q1 2026**. By contrast, a five-flight change in average remaining flights for flight vehicles/spacecraft would *not* be material — underscoring that satellite useful-life assumptions are far more impactful to earnings than launch vehicle assumptions.

3. **Replenishment cost pressure** — Connectivity cost of revenue increased by **$1,153 million** in 2025, driven by "higher depreciation as the number of satellites placed into orbit grew and higher operating expenses." The filing also cites "depreciation of capitalized launch and satellite costs due to the increase in Starlink flights" as a recurring headwind to Connectivity operating income.

4. **Capitalized launch costs flow through depreciation** — As Starlink satellite launches increased from 89 in 2024 to 122 in 2025, a relatively greater share of launch operations and overhead costs were capitalized into the Connectivity segment, shifting costs from period expense to depreciated asset.

5. **Technology evolution risk** — The Company explicitly considers "planned transitions to newer satellite technology" and "the evolution of constellation density and technology" in setting useful lives, acknowledging that generational obsolescence can shorten economic lives.

## Total Consolidated Depreciation and Amortization — FY2025

Two figures are disclosed, depending on scope:

| Metric | FY2025 Amount |
|---|---|
| **Depreciation expense** (PP&E only) | **$5,915 million** |
| **Depreciation and amortization** (segment supplemental, includes intangibles) | **$6,701 million** |

The $6,701 million segment-level D&A breaks down as follows:

| Segment | D&A (FY2025) |
|---|---|
| Space | $757 million |
| Connectivity | $2,376 million |
| AI | $3,568 million |
| **Total Reportable Segments** | **$6,701 million** |

The ~$786 million difference between the two figures ($6,701M − $5,915M) is attributable to **amortization of finite-lived intangible assets** (which had a net carrying value of $1,537 million as of December 31, 2025) and potentially other non-PP&E amortization included in the segment supplemental disclosure.

arguments.prompt: In {{viasat_10k_2025}}, what is the satellite depreciation expense for the satellite/space segment? What is the satellite segment revenue? What is the segment operating income (or loss) for the satellite segment? Please provide the exact figures and any reconciliation between segment operating income and EBITDA. Also, what does the filing say about satellite useful lives and depreciation policies?

results.content:
# Response to Satellite Segment Inquiry

## Important Clarification: Viasat Does Not Report a Standalone "Satellite/Space Segment"

Based on the filing excerpts provided, Viasat reports **two operating segments only**:
1. **Communication Services** (which contains the company's satellite operations, including aviation, government, maritime, and fixed services)
2. **Defense and Advanced Technologies**

There is no separately disclosed "satellite segment" or "space segment." Satellite assets, depreciation, and related revenues are embedded within the **Communication Services segment**. The figures below therefore reflect that segment.

---

## 1. Depreciation Expense (Communication Services Segment — FY2025)

| Fiscal Year Ended March 31, | 2025 | 2024 | 2023 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Communication Services Depreciation | **$988,447 thousand** (~$988.4 million) | $825,788 thousand | $350,954 thousand |
| Defense and Advanced Technologies Depreciation | $48,020 thousand | $41,853 thousand | $52,701 thousand |

Note: This depreciation line is not broken out between satellites, earth stations, networking equipment, and CPE at the segment level. However, the filing separately discloses CPE carrying values (see below).

### CPE Units (Customer Premises Equipment) — Disclosed Separately Within Property, Equipment and Satellites, Net

| As of March 31, | Total Cost | Accumulated Depreciation | Net Book Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2025 | $526.0 million | $328.0 million | $198.0 million |
| 2024 | $567.5 million | $267.4 million | $300.1 million |

---

## 2. Communication Services Segment Revenue (FY2025)

| Communication Services Revenue | FY2025 | FY2024 | FY2023 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aviation services | $1,048,225 | $864,834 | $346,950 |
| Government satcom services | $754,552 | $596,826 | $165,897 |
| Maritime services | $477,968 | $430,090 | $3,362 |
| Fixed services and other services | $741,601 | $906,762 | $919,440 |
| **Total services** | $3,022,346 | $2,798,512 | $1,435,649 |
| Total products | $276,139 | $343,028 | $269,126 |
| **Total communication services revenues** | **$3,298,485 thousand** (~$3,298.5 million) | $3,141,540 | $1,704,775 |

(All figures in thousands of USD.)

---

## 3. Segment Operating Income (Loss) — Communication Services

| Segment Operating Profit (Loss) | FY2025 | FY2024 | FY2023 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Communication Services** | **$(50,222) thousand** (~$(50.2) million) | $(817,054) thousand | $(189,402) thousand |
| Defense and Advanced Technologies | $216,675 | $154,413 | $63,257 |
| Elimination of intersegment | — | — | — |

### Drivers cited in the MD&A for the FY2025 vs. FY2024 improvement:
- **Prior-year satellite impairment of ~$905.5 million** (FY2024) did not recur
- Higher earnings contributions of **$40.7 million** from increased service revenues (Inmarsat Acquisition)
- **$21.5 million decrease** in IR&D expenses (next-gen consumer broadband)
- Partially offset by:
  - **$169.4 million ground network impairment** (EMEA exit, Q4 FY2025)
  - **$31.5 million increase** in SG&A (full year of Inmarsat vs. 10 months in prior year)

---

## 4. Reconciliation Between Segment Operating Profit and Pre-Tax Income (Not EBITDA)

The filing does **not** present an explicit EBITDA reconciliation in the chunks provided. Instead, the segment operating profit is reconciled to **Income (loss) from continuing operations before income taxes** through the following items (line items identified in the segment note):

- Segment operating profit (loss) before corporate and amortization of acquired intangible assets
- Amortization of acquired intangible assets
- (Loss) gain on extinguishment of debt, net
- Income (loss) from continuing operations before income taxes

A user wishing to construct an EBITDA proxy would need to add back:
- Depreciation (Communication Services: $988.4M FY2025)
- Amortization of acquired intangible assets
- Interest expense (net)
- Taxes
- Impairments ($169.4M FY2025 ground network; $905.5M FY2024 satellite)

However, **the filing does not explicitly present this as a defined EBITDA measure** in the excerpts provided.

---

## 5. Satellite Useful Lives and Depreciation Policies

### Estimated Useful Lives (from the property and equipment note)

| Asset Category | Estimated Useful Life |
|---|---|
| Equipment and software | 3 – 7 years |
| CPE leased equipment | 4 – 7 years |
| Furniture and fixtures | 7 years |
| Leasehold improvements | 2 – 20 years |
| Buildings | 12 – 38 years |
| **Satellites** | **7 – 17 years** |
| **Satellite Ka-band capacity obtained under finance leases** | **7 – 15 years** |

### Capitalization Policy (from Significant Accounting Policies)

> "Satellites and other property and equipment, including internally developed software, are recorded at cost or, in the case of certain satellites and other property acquired, the fair value at the date of acquisition, net of accumulated depreciation."

**Capitalized satellite costs include:**
- Costs of satellite construction and launch
- Launch insurance
- Insurance during the period of in-orbit testing
- Net present value of performance incentive payments expected to be payable to satellite manufacturers (dependent on continued satisfactory performance)
- Costs directly associated with monitoring and support of satellite construction
- Interest costs incurred during the period of satellite construction

**Other capitalized costs:**
- Earth stations, network operations systems, and other supporting assets — construction costs, including interest, are capitalized as incurred.

### Useful Life Estimation Methodology

> "At the time satellites are placed in commercial service, the Company estimates the useful life of its satellites for depreciation purposes based upon an analysis of each satellite's performance against the original manufacturer's orbital design life, estimated fuel levels and related consumption rates, as well as historical satellite operating trends."

The Company **periodically reviews** the remaining estimated useful life of its satellites to determine if revisions are necessary.

### Additions, Maintenance, and Disposals

- **Capitalized:** Additions, major renewals, and betterments — depreciated over the remaining life of the underlying asset.
- **Expensed:** Maintenance, repairs, and minor renewals and betterments — charged to expense as incurred.
- **Disposals:** Cost and related accumulated depreciation/amortization are removed; any gain or loss is recognized in operations.

### Satellite Launch Cycle Impacts (from MD&A)

The filing explicitly warns that new satellite launches (including the ViaSat-3 constellation) create a **cyclical pattern** of:
- Increased operating costs during ramp-up (depreciation, amortization of capitalized software, earth station connectivity, marketing, customer care, support systems)
- Increased interest expense once the satellite is in commercial service (interest is no longer capitalized)
- Capacity constraints on existing satellites in the lead-up to new satellite commercial service
- Subsequent revenue base expansion and operating cost efficiencies as services scale

## SpaceX Connectivity Segment: Implied D&A Add-Back vs. Viasat Satellite Depreciation — LEO vs. GEO Structural Analysis

### 1. SpaceX Connectivity Segment FY2025 EBITDA Bridge

From the SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026), the Connectivity segment reconciliation for the year ended December 31, 2025 is:

| (in $ millions) | Connectivity |
|---|---|
| Income from operations (Segment Operating Income) | $4,423 |
| Add: Depreciation and amortization | **$2,376** |
| Add: Share-based compensation | $369 |
| Add: Restructuring charges | $0 |
| Add: Impairment | $0 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$7,168** |

**Implied D&A add-back = $7,168 − $4,423 − $369 = $2,376 million**

Verification: $4,423 + $2,376 + $369 = $7,168 ✓

Connectivity segment revenue (FY2025): **$11,387 million** (primarily Starlink).

**SpaceX Connectivity D&A as % of segment revenue = $2,376 / $11,387 = 20.87%**

The S-1 also discloses that SpaceX depreciates **broadband satellites over a 5-year useful life** and **first-generation mobile satellites over a 3-year useful life**, and that a one-year change in the average useful life of satellite assets would have an approximately **$480 million impact on FY2025 operating income** — confirming that satellite depreciation is the dominant driver of the D&A line.

---

### 2. Viasat Most Recent 10-K (FY2025, fiscal year ended March 31, 2025) — Satellite Depreciation Disclosure

**Important disclosure caveat:** Viasat's most recent 10-K does **not** report a standalone "space segment" and does **not** separately disclose a "satellite depreciation" line item. Viasat reports two operating segments — **Communication Services** (which contains all satellite operations) and **Defense and Advanced Technologies**. Satellite depreciation is embedded within total Communication Services depreciation. The question's premise that Viasat discloses satellite depreciation as a discrete line item is not supported by the filing; the best available proxy is total Communication Services depreciation.

| (in $ millions) | Viasat Communication Services FY2025 |
|---|---|
| Segment revenue | $3,298.5 |
| Segment depreciation expense (total, all assets) | $988.4 |
| Segment operating income (loss) | $(50.2) |
| Satellite useful life range | 7 – 17 years |
| Satellite Ka-band finance-lease capacity useful life | 7 – 15 years |
| Satellites in service — gross cost | $3,405.1 |
| Satellite Ka-band capacity (finance leases) — gross cost | $338.2 |
| Satellites under construction — gross cost | $2,205.3 |

**Viasat total Communication Services depreciation / segment revenue = $988.4 / $3,298.5 = 29.97%**

Because Viasat does not break out satellite-specific depreciation, an estimate can be derived from gross satellite cost and the midpoint useful life (~12 years for in-service satellites; ~11 years for Ka-band capacity):

**Estimated satellite-only depreciation ≈ ($3,405.1 + $338.2) / 12 ≈ $312 million**

**Estimated satellite depreciation / Communication Services revenue ≈ $312 / $3,298.5 ≈ 9.46%**

---

### 3. Directional Comparison

| Metric | SpaceX Connectivity (LEO) | Viasat Comm. Services (GEO) |
|---|---|---|
| D&A / segment revenue (total) | **20.87%** | **29.97%** (total depreciation, not satellite-only) |
| Estimated satellite-only depreciation / revenue | ~20.9% (D&A is overwhelmingly satellite-driven) | **~9.46%** (estimated) |
| Satellite useful life | **5 years** (broadband) / 3 years (mobile) | **7 – 17 years** |
| Segment operating income | **$4,423M (positive)** | **$(50.2)M (loss)** |
| Implied segment EBITDA (rough) | $7,168M | ~$938M (loss + depreciation) |
| EBITDA-to-Operating-Income conversion | **61.7%** ($4,423 / $7,168) | **−5.4%** (negative — operating loss despite positive EBITDA) |

**Key directional findings:**

- **Satellite-specific depreciation intensity is structurally higher for LEO.** SpaceX's Connectivity D&A (~20.9% of revenue) is overwhelmingly satellite-driven given the 5-year useful life and continuous replenishment, whereas Viasat's estimated satellite-only depreciation is roughly half that (~9.5% of revenue) because GEO satellites are depreciated over 7–17 years. The shorter LEO useful life mechanically doubles (or more) the annual depreciation burden per dollar of in-orbit asset.

- **Viasat's *total* segment depreciation is actually higher than SpaceX's** (29.97% vs. 20.87%) — but this is driven by Viasat's ground infrastructure, earth stations, CPE, and a heavy ViaSat-3 investment cycle, not by satellite depreciation per se. This confirms that the satellite-depreciation component specifically is lower for GEO, even though Viasat's overall capital intensity is currently elevated.

- **The EBITDA-to-operating-income conversion gap is dramatically different.** SpaceX converts 61.7% of Segment Adjusted EBITDA into operating income. Viasat's Communication Services segment shows a **negative** conversion: a ~$988M depreciation add-back flips a $50M operating loss into roughly $938M of implied EBITDA. The depreciation charge is approximately 20× the segment's operating loss.

---

### 4. What This Reveals About LEO vs. GEO Satellite Depreciation Profiles

**LEO constellation economics (SpaceX/Starlink):**
- **Shorter useful lives (5 years vs. 7–17 years)** produce a structurally higher annual depreciation rate per dollar of in-orbit asset.
- **Higher satellite counts (thousands vs. a handful)** mean a much larger gross asset base is simultaneously depreciating.
- **Continuous replenishment** (122 Starlink launches in 2025, up from 89 in 2024) means the depreciation charge is a **permanent, steady-state feature** of the P&L — it never declines because retired satellites are continuously replaced by newly capitalized ones. The S-1 explicitly attributes the $1,153M increase in Connectivity cost of revenue to "higher depreciation as the number of satellites placed into orbit grew."
- **Useful-life sensitivity is extreme:** a one-year change in satellite useful life swings operating income by ~$480M (FY2025), underscoring that the depreciation assumption is the single most material accounting estimate for LEO operators.

**GEO satellite depreciation profiles (Viasat):**
- **Longer useful lives (7–17 years)** spread capitalized satellite cost over a much longer period, producing lower annual satellite depreciation per dollar of asset.
- **Fewer, larger satellites** mean depreciation is **lumpy and cyclical** — it spikes when a new satellite (e.g., ViaSat-3) enters commercial service, then runs off over 7–17 years. Between replacement cycles, depreciation can decline materially.
- **Impairment risk dominates over depreciation timing:** Viasat's recent segment losses were driven by a ~$905.5M satellite impairment (FY2024) and a $169.4M ground network impairment (FY2025), not by steady-state depreciation — a different failure mode than LEO.

---

### 5. Why EBITDA-to-Operating-Income Conversion Is Critical for Evaluating LEO Broadband Operators

The comparison reveals several structural reasons why EBITDA is a particularly misleading profitability metric for LEO broadband operators as a class:

1. **Perpetual capital intensity is hidden by EBITDA.** Unlike GEO operators — where depreciation eventually runs off between replacement cycles — LEO operators face a **permanent depreciation charge** because the constellation must be continuously replenished. EBITDA adds back depreciation as if it were a non-recurring non-cash item, but for LEO it represents a recurring economic cost (satellites must be rebuilt and relaunched). The $2,376M D&A add-back in SpaceX's Connectivity bridge is not a one-time accounting artifact; it is the steady-state cost of maintaining a 5-year-life constellation.

2. **Useful-life assumptions are the dominant value driver.** SpaceX's disclosure that a one-year useful-life change moves operating income by ~$480M means that EBITDA — which is invariant to useful-life assumptions — strips out precisely the variable that most determines true LEO profitability. Two LEO operators with identical EBITDA can have vastly different operating incomes depending on whether they assume 4, 5, or 6-year satellite lives.

3. **The EBITDA-to-OI gap is structural, not transitional.** For Viasat, the gap between positive EBITDA and negative operating income reflects a combination of cyclical factors (impairments, investment ramp) that may eventually reverse. For LEO operators, the gap is **structural and permanent** — it will persist as long as the constellation is being replenished, because depreciation will always approximate the cost of replacing the retiring fleet.

4. **Scale and cost efficiency determine whether the structural gap is survivable.** SpaceX's ability to generate $4,423M of positive Connectivity operating income despite a 20.87% D&A-to-revenue ratio demonstrates that LEO economics can work — but only when (a) revenue scales faster than the depreciation base (49.8% YoY revenue growth in 2025), (b) per-satellite manufacturing and launch costs decline, and (c) the constellation reaches sufficient density that revenue per satellite exceeds depreciation per satellite. Viasat's negative operating income, despite a lower satellite-specific depreciation rate, shows that GEO operators face a different problem (sub-scale revenue relative to a lumpy, impairment-prone asset base).

5. **Implication for peer benchmarking.** When evaluating LEO broadband operators (SpaceX/Starlink, potential peers such as Project Kuiper, OneWeb), investors should anchor on **operating income or free cash flow**, not EBITDA, because:
   - EBITDA systematically overstates LEO profitability by ignoring perpetual replenishment capex;
   - Useful-life assumptions — invisible in EBITDA — are the most material accounting estimate;
   - The EBITDA-to-OI conversion ratio itself is a diagnostic: a low or negative conversion ratio signals that the operator has not yet achieved the scale or cost efficiency needed to cover its structural depreciation burden.

---

### Summary

SpaceX's Connectivity segment carries an implied D&A add-back of **$2,376 million** (20.87% of segment revenue) in its FY2025 EBITDA bridge, driven by 5-year satellite useful lives and continuous replenishment. Viasat's most recent 10-K does not separately disclose satellite depreciation, but total Communication Services depreciation is $988.4 million (29.97% of segment revenue), with estimated satellite-only depreciation of roughly $312 million (~9.46% of revenue) given 7–17 year GEO useful lives. The comparison confirms that **LEO satellite depreciation is structurally more intense per dollar of revenue than GEO satellite depreciation**, but SpaceX has achieved positive operating income (61.7% EBITDA conversion) through scale and cost efficiency, while Viasat's segment shows negative operating income despite positive EBITDA. This makes EBITDA-to-operating-income conversion the single most important diagnostic when evaluating LEO broadband operators, because the depreciation gap is permanent, useful-life-sensitive, and masks the perpetual capital intensity that defines the LEO business model.

2. **Useful-life assumptions are the dominant value driver.** SpaceX's disclosure that a one-year useful-life change moves operating income by ~$480M means that EBITDA — which is invariant to useful-life assumptions — strips out precisely the variable that most determines true LEO profitability. Two LEO operators with identical EBITDA can have vastly different operating incomes depending on whether they assume 4, 5, or 6-year satellite lives.

3. **The EBITDA-to-OI gap is structural, not transitional.** For Viasat, the gap between positive EBITDA and negative operating income reflects a combination of cyclical factors (impairments, investment ramp) that may eventually reverse. For LEO operators, the gap is **structural and permanent** — it will persist as long as the constellation is being replenished, because depreciation will always approximate the cost of replacing the retiring fleet.

4. **Scale and cost efficiency determine whether the structural gap is survivable.** SpaceX's ability to generate $4,423M of positive Connectivity operating income despite a 20.87% D&A-to-revenue ratio demonstrates that LEO economics can work — but only when (a) revenue scales faster than the depreciation base (49.8% YoY revenue growth in 2025), (b) per-satellite manufacturing and launch costs decline, and (c) the constellation reaches sufficient density that revenue per satellite exceeds depreciation per satellite. Viasat's negative operating income, despite a lower satellite-specific depreciation rate, shows that GEO operators face a different problem (sub-scale revenue relative to a lumpy, impairment-prone asset base).

5. **Implication for peer benchmarking.** When evaluating LEO broadband operators (SpaceX/Starlink, potential peers such as Project Kuiper, OneWeb), investors should anchor on **operating income or free cash flow**, not EBITDA, because:
   - EBITDA systematically overstates LEO profitability by ignoring perpetual replenishment capex;
   - Useful-life assumptions — invisible in EBITDA — are the most material accounting estimate;
   - The EBITDA-to-OI conversion ratio itself is a diagnostic: a low or negative conversion ratio signals that the operator has not yet achieved the scale or cost efficiency needed to cover its structural depreciation burden.

---

### Summary

SpaceX's Connectivity segment carries an implied D&A add-back of **$2,376 million** (20.87% of segment revenue) in its FY2025 EBITDA bridge, driven by 5-year satellite useful lives and continuous replenishment. Viasat's most recent 10-K does not separately disclose satellite depreciation, but total Communication Services depreciation is $988.4 million (29.97% of segment revenue), with estimated satellite-only depreciation of roughly $312 million (~9.46% of revenue) given 7–17 year GEO useful lives. The comparison confirms that **LEO satellite depreciation is structurally more intense per dollar of revenue than GEO satellite depreciation**, but SpaceX has achieved positive operating income (61.7% EBITDA conversion) through scale and cost efficiency, while Viasat's segment shows negative operating income despite positive EBITDA. This makes EBITDA-to-operating-income conversion the single most important diagnostic when evaluating LEO broadband operators, because the depreciation gap is permanent, useful-life-sensitive, and masks the perpetual capital intensity that defines the LEO business model.
